tables.title: The Four Categories of Pediatric Respiratory Compromise
rows: Inspiratory stridor, barking cough, hoarseness, drooling, tripod position, muffled voice
Croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis), epiglottitis, foreign body above the cords, peritonsillar abscess
Croup: dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg + racemic epinephrine nebulizer; Epiglottitis: secure airway; Foreign body: remove
Expiratory wheezing, prolonged expiratory phase, air trapping, hyperinflation on CXR, diminished air entry
Albuterol nebulizer 2.5 mg q20 min × 3, ipratropium if severe, supplemental O2, systemic corticosteroids
Crackles (rales), dullness to percussion, hypoxemia out of proportion to effort, radiographic infiltrates/edema
Bacterial pneumonia, viral pneumonia, pulmonary edema, ARDS, aspiration pneumonitis
Supplemental O2 targeting SpO2 ≥94%, antibiotics within 1 hour if bacterial suspected, diuretics for edema
Abnormal respiratory pattern (irregular, apneic, Cheyne-Stokes, shallow), clear lung auscultation, neurological signs
Raised ICP, traumatic brain injury, opioid/sedative toxicity, encephalopathy, neuromuscular disease (GBS, muscular dystrophy)
Airway support (intubation + mechanical ventilation), naloxone for opioid cause, treat underlying neurological condition

tables.title: Inspiratory Stridor vs Expiratory Wheeze
rows: Croup, epiglottitis, foreign body, bacterial tracheitis
Asthma, bronchiolitis, foreign body (if lodged lower)
Loud over the neck, loudest at the suprasternal notch
Diffuse, heard best over the anterior chest with forced expiration
Reduce supraglottic edema (dexa, racemic epi) or relieve mechanical obstruction

tables.title: Respiratory Distress vs Respiratory Failure
rows: May be increased then suddenly decreased (fatigue)
